content:
Ryan, I’d put my arm around my 10‑year‑old while Psalm 19 is read and quietly help them love what they’re hearing.

- In the moment: “Listen—this is poetry, but poetry in the Bible tells the truth. The sky really does ‘declare’ God’s glory. It’s a picture to help us feel what’s true. When it says the sun runs its course, that’s how it looks to us every day. The Bible often speaks that way and it’s still completely true.”

- On the way home: “Poetry doesn’t mean pretend; it means packed truth. Psalm 19 and Genesis 1 agree: God made everything by His word, and creation shouts about Him (see Romans 1:20). Some people call Genesis 1 ‘poetic’ because it’s beautifully structured, but it also reads like history God wants us to trust. I believe God really created in six days because Scripture treats it that way (Exodus 20:11), but Christians who love Jesus sometimes differ on how to understand the ‘days.’ What we never do is change how we read the Bible just to fit in. We let the Bible tell us how to read it, and we trust every word.”

- Midweek with the elder (graciously, privately): “I share your desire for unity. Could we seek unity around what the text actually says rather than assign Genesis 1 a genre for the sake of peace? Poetry is true speech, but Genesis 1 bears the marks of exalted narrative. Even if we give room for members who see the days differently, can we be clear that Scripture is inerrant and authoritative, and that our hermeneutics flow from the text, not from pressure to avoid being a ‘stumbling block’? The gospel will offend; we should be careful not to remove offense by dulling the Scriptures’ clarity.”

With your child, keep reinforcing:
- All Scripture is God-breathed and without error.
- Genre shapes how we read, not whether we trust.
- Creation really declares God’s glory, and Genesis really tells us what God did.

And keep modeling charity: hold convictions firmly, speak gently, and let the Word lead the church into unity in the truth.
